# Hybrid Simulator: QEMU + Verilator (vsim)

A plan to extend `vsim` from a pure Verilator cycle-accurate simulator into a hybrid functional/RTL simulator that runs the boring code in QEMU at MHz speed and switches into Verilator only for the cycle-accurate region of interest (ROI), then optionally hands back.

---

## 1. Context

`vsim` runs at 5-9 KHz on Andes RISC-V cores. A typical workload spends >99% of cycles in boot, libc init, and warm-up before reaching the kernel under study. Today the only escape hatch is `--save`/`--continue`, which still requires an initial RTL run to reach the snapshot point. There is also already a `--kanata-loop-{start,end,skip,record}` mechanism (`src/main.cpp:183-214`) that identifies the interesting loop iterations.

We want: run everything before the ROI in QEMU (functional, MHz), hand the architectural state to Verilator at the ROI boundary, run the ROI cycle-accurately to record kanata/clarity pipeline data, and optionally hand back to QEMU to finish.

The intended outcome: 100x-1000x reduction in wall-clock time for benchmarking workflows that only care about steady-state pipeline behavior of a small kernel, with byte-identical kanata output for the recorded window vs. a fully-RTL baseline.

---

## 2. Tutorial: Why This Is Technically Feasible

### 2.1 The state-exchange problem, reframed

A naive hybrid sim has to "transfer" CPU state between two simulators. In practice the state splits into three categories with very different costs:

| Category | Items | Size | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architectural regs | x0..x31, f0..f31, V regs, key CSRs | 4-8 KB | **Copy** via well-defined ABI struct |
| Memory | guest RAM, ILM/DLM | MB-GB | **Share** via `mmap` - zero copy |
| Microarchitectural state | TLB, caches, BPU, store buffer | KB-MB | **Discard** - let cold starts repopulate |

The key trick: **memory does not move**. QEMU runs with `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=/dev/shm/vsim-ram`. vsim's `SimpleMemory::data` (`src/platform/simple_memory.hpp:20`) is changed to an `mmap` of the same fd. Both simulators read and write the same physical bytes. The same trick is used by QEMU's CPR (`docs/devel/migration/CPR.rst`) and gem5's `SimpleSwitchableProcessor`. RAM size becomes irrelevant.

Microarch state is intentionally discarded. We accept cold caches/BPU on entry into Verilator and rely on the existing `kanata_loop_skip N` mechanism to warm them up before recording. This matches the gem5 "switch + warmup" pattern.

### 2.2 The trigger: a magic CSR convention

We need both sides to agree on "the ROI starts here." Reuse the user-defined CSR space at `0x7C0`:

```asm
csrwi 0x7C0, 0   # enter detail (QEMU -> vsim)
csrwi 0x7C0, 1   # exit detail  (vsim -> QEMU)
```

- **QEMU** sees this via a TCG plugin: `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb()` with a per-instruction filter that pattern-matches the encoding of `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` (mask `0xfff03073` against the 32-bit insn). On hit, the plugin drains state and exits.
- **vsim** sees this via the existing retire signal taps in `src/signals/common_signals_45.hpp`. We pre-scan the ELF for PCs containing `csrw 0x7C0, *` (LIEF is already linked - `src/utils/elf_loader.hpp`), then watch for retire-of-PC against that set.

Phase 4 lifts this further: `--kanata-loop-start <pc>` and `--kanata-loop-end <pc>` already specify the ROI by PC. The orchestrator wraps these as PC-trigger events and **the binary needs no magic CSR at all**.

We deliberately avoid `tohost`/`fromhost` (HTIF reserved) and `ecall` (already used by the runtime).

### 2.3 The write-back path: existing JTAG/DTM, in-process

This is the highest-leverage finding. vsim already has:

- `src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp` - a full JTAG DTM implementation with the RISC-V Debug Module
- `--jtag-port` CLI flag and OpenOCD support (`src/main.cpp:148-154`)

The RISC-V Debug Spec gives Abstract Commands that read/write GPRs (`regno` 0x1000-0x101F), FPRs (0x1020-0x103F), and CSRs (0x0000-0x0FFF) directly via the Debug Module. **Writing all 32 GPRs + 32 FPRs + ~20 critical CSRs is ~85 DMI transactions**, which is ~hundreds of cycles - negligible compared to the millions of cycles we save.

We drive the DTM **in-process**, calling its `handle_dmi_*` methods directly without an OpenOCD socket. No DPI shim, no Verilog modifications, no per-CPU-variant code (works for all ten CPU wrappers in `src/cpu_wrappers/`).

The sequence to "resume from QEMU state":

1. Reset CPU; halt request via DM
2. For each register in the ABI struct, issue Abstract Command (`regno` + data)
3. Set DPC = saved PC
4. Issue `dret` via Program Buffer

Vector registers and any CPU-specific custom CSR that DM doesn't expose are deferred to Phase 5. If DM coverage is incomplete we add a per-CPU Program Buffer fallback or, last resort, an mmap-shared V regfile via DPI.

### 2.4 The QEMU side: a TCG plugin against the existing build

`/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64` is QEMU **9.2.0** (verified). This is recent enough for the full plugin API:

- `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb()` - per-insn callback
- `qemu_plugin_get_registers()` / `qemu_plugin_read_register()` - drain GPR/FP/CSR
- `qemu_plugin_request_exit()` - stop QEMU cleanly

Templates already on disk: `/local/nick/qemu_v5/contrib/plugins/{execlog.c, hotblocks.c, bbv.c}`. We add one new plugin `hybrid_handoff.c` next to them. No QEMU patches, no fork.

### 2.5 What does NOT work and is out of scope

- **MMIO inside the ROI**: device state lives in QEMU C and has no mirror in RTL. Document; fail loudly via an interconnect watch in Phase 5.
- **lr/sc reservations across handoff**: invalidate at handoff; do not transfer.
- **Self-modifying code in the ROI**: the magic CSR is a synchronization boundary by convention.
- **mcycle/minstret continuity**: monotonic across handoffs but not equal to a fully-RTL run; documented discontinuity.
- **Andes-internal QEMU fork** (`andescycle-dev-v0.16-17-g03dfe23.tar.gz`): treated as opaque. Plan against mainline QEMU 9.2.

---

## 3. Architecture at a Glance

```
                              shared mmap RAM (/dev/shm/vsim-ram)
                                           |
              +----------------------------+----------------------------+
              |                                                         |
              v                                                         v
       QEMU 9.2.0 + plugin                                    vsim (SystemC + Verilator)
       +---------------------+                                +-----------------------+
       | TCG insn loop       |     state.bin (ABI struct)     | SystemC main loop     |
       | hybrid_handoff.so   |  <-->  [ABI v1 packed]   <-->  | jtag_dtm in-process   |
       | csrw 0x7C0 -> drain |                                | csr_trigger -> drain  |
       +---------------------+                                +-----------------------+
              ^                                                         ^
              |                                                         |
              +--orchestrator script (vsim-hybrid)----------------------+
                  enter-pc / exit-pc mode also supported
```

State exchange contract: a single packed `struct hybrid_state_v1` (header + GPR + FPR + CSR + V) defined in `hybrid/include/state_abi.h`, written atomically (`O_TMPFILE` + `linkat`) at handoff.

---

## 4. Design Decisions (locked)

| # | Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| a | **JTAG/DTM in-process** for register write-back (vs. DPI shim or synthetic insn stream) | Existing infrastructure (`src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp`); zero RTL changes; works across all 10 CPU wrappers |
| b | Same for CSRs; Program Buffer fallback for unreachable ones | Same |
| c | **Modify `SimpleMemory` in-place**: backing becomes `std::span<uint8_t>` with optional mmap; existing `std::vector` path retained when mmap unset | All call sites use `.data()`/`.size()` only - both span and vector satisfy. Templated class used as concrete member; forking is worse |
| d | **TCG plugin** (in-process, fastest) against existing QEMU 9.2.0 build at `/local/nick/qemu_v5` | Plugin API is feature-complete in 9.x; templates available; no fork |
| e | **Packed C struct** in shared file (vs. protobuf/JSON) | C-and-C++-compatible single header; zero parse cost; static_assert offsets to catch drift |

---

## 5. Phased Implementation Plan

Each phase lands a working subset and is independently testable.

### Phase 0 - Plumbing, validation, ABI freeze

**Goal**: skeleton in place, riskiest assumptions de-risked, no runtime behavior change.

Deliverables:
- `hybrid/include/state_abi.h` - versioned packed struct: magic `'AHYB'`, version, flags, pc, priv, gpr[32], fpr[32], M-mode CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mtvec, mcause, mtval, mscratch, medeleg, mideleg, mip, mie, mcycle, minstret, satp, pmpcfg[16], pmpaddr[64]), V state (vstart, vtype, vl, vlenb, vreg[32 * MAX_VLEN_BYTES]), 256B trailing reserved.
- `hybrid/include/state_abi_check.h` - `static_assert` on offsets and size.
- `cmake/HybridConfig.cmake` - adds `VSIM_QEMU_ROOT` cache var (default `/local/nick/qemu_v5`), build option `VSIM_ENABLE_HYBRID=ON`.
- `hybrid/scripts/test_dm_coverage.tcl` - 50-line OpenOCD script connecting to vsim's DM, probing which `regno` ranges return success. **De-risks the JTAG decision.** Document output in `hybrid/docs/dm_coverage.md`.

Test: vsim builds with `-DVSIM_ENABLE_HYBRID=ON`, plugin builds against `/local/nick/qemu_v5`, struct `static_assert`s pass.

Risk to escalate: if DM Abstract Commands cannot reach vector regs / Andes-custom CSRs, Phase 5 must add Program Buffer or DPI - decide before starting Phase 2.

### Phase 1 - Shared mmap RAM

**Goal**: vsim runs identically to today, but main memory is mmap-backed when `--shared-mem-path` is set.

Files modified:
- `src/platform/simple_memory.hpp:20` - `std::vector<uint8_t> data` becomes a `std::span<uint8_t> data` plus an optional owning `std::vector<uint8_t> owned_storage_` plus a `bool owns_mapping_` flag. Add a constructor taking `(int fd, size_t offset, size_t size)` that mmaps in.
- `src/platform/platform.hpp` - thread mmap fd from `Simulator` into `main_memory` construction via a `std::optional<MmapBacking>` member.
- `src/main.cpp` - new flags `--shared-mem-path <file>`, `--shared-mem-size <bytes>`. When unset, behave as today.

Files NOT modified (already span-compatible): `src/utils/elf_loader.hpp`, `src/simulator_context.hpp` (uses `.data()`/`.size()` only), `src/simulator.hpp:190,228-230`.

### Phase 2 - One-way handoff: QEMU -> vsim

**Goal**: program runs under QEMU, hits `csrwi 0x7C0, 0`, QEMU drains state and exits. vsim is invoked with `--resume-from-qemu <state.bin>` and runs from the resumed PC to program end.

New files:
- `hybrid/qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` - modeled on `/local/nick/qemu_v5/contrib/plugins/execlog.c`. TB callback walks insns; per-insn callback registered only on `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` matches. On fire, dispatch on `rs1` value (0=enter, 1=exit), drain GPR/FP/CSR via `qemu_plugin_read_register()`, write packed struct atomically, call `qemu_plugin_request_exit()`.
- `hybrid/qemu_plugin/meson.build` - builds `libhybrid_handoff.so`.
- `src/hybrid/qemu_resume.hpp` - reads ABI struct, validates magic/version, drives DM via `jtag_dtm` directly (no socket): halt -> for each reg issue Abstract Command -> set DPC -> `dret`.

Files modified:
- `src/main.cpp` - new flag `--resume-from-qemu <file>`, mutually exclusive with `--continue`.
- `src/simulator.hpp:239` - `reset()` extended with "resume from external state" branch that runs after `resetn` is asserted; reuses the existing JTAG infrastructure in-process.

Test: tiny RV64 program:
```asm
_start:
  csrwi 0x7C0, 0
  li a0, 1; li a1, 2; add a2, a0, a1
  li a7, 93; ecall
```
Compile, run under QEMU+plugin, observe state file. Run vsim with `--resume-from-qemu state.bin`, verify `a2 == 3` at retire and exit code 0.

### Phase 3 - Round-trip: vsim -> QEMU

**Goal**: vsim detects exit-detail magic CSR write, drains state, hands back to QEMU on the same shared RAM.

New files:
- `src/hybrid/csr_trigger.hpp` - `CsrTrigger` class. ELF pre-scan computes the set of PCs containing `csrw 0x7C0, *`. Subscribes to retire signals (`wb_i0_pc`/`wb_i0_retire` from `src/signals/common_signals_45.hpp`). On retire-of-PC in the set, decode the saved insn to get `rs1` register, read GPR via existing probe (`gpr_index_hart0`/`gpr_value_hart0` in `src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:91-92`). If value=1, signal handoff.
- `src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp` - dual of `qemu_resume.hpp`: halt CPU, read all archregs via DM Abstract Commands, populate ABI struct, msync to file.

Files modified:
- `src/simulator.hpp:289-340` (the `run()` loop) - add `csr_trigger.tick(cpu)` after the per-cycle checks. On drain signal, break loop with new exit code `EXIT_HANDOFF_TO_QEMU = 200`.
- `src/main.cpp` - on `EXIT_HANDOFF_TO_QEMU` plus `--qemu-binary` set, after `sim.cpu.final()` (line 342), `execve` QEMU with `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=<shared-mem-path>` and `-plugin libhybrid_handoff.so,resume_from=<state.bin>`. The plugin's `resume_from` mode pokes state back via `qemu_plugin_write_register()` at the first TB callback.

Test: extend Phase 2 program with `csrwi 0x7C0, 1; ecall` after the body. Run vsim with `--resume-from-qemu state_in.bin --qemu-binary /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64`. Verify the QEMU side processes the ecall and returns 0.

### Phase 4 - Kanata-loop integration

**Goal**: existing `--kanata-loop-start <pc> --kanata-loop-end <pc>` flags drive hybrid mode automatically. **No magic CSR required in the binary.**

New files:
- `hybrid/scripts/vsim-hybrid` (Python orchestrator):
  1. Inspect ELF + user's loop PCs.
  2. Launch QEMU with hybrid plugin in **PC-trigger mode** (`enter_pc=<start>`, `exit_pc=<end>` plugin args).
  3. On QEMU exit, launch vsim in resume mode with state file + the kanata flags unchanged.
  4. On vsim's exit-detail, optionally re-launch QEMU to finish.

Files modified:
- `hybrid/qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` - PC-trigger mode added; cheap with `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb()`.
- `src/hybrid/csr_trigger.hpp` - sibling `PcTrigger` class for `--hybrid-enter-pc` / `--hybrid-exit-pc` CLI flags.
- `src/main.cpp` - `--hybrid-mode=auto` aliases `--kanata-loop-start/end` to the hybrid PC triggers.

Test: boot a benchmark from `external/ax45mpv_premium/testbench/`, hand off at the steady-state inner loop, record kanata for N iterations in vsim, hand back to QEMU. The recorded kanata file for the captured iterations must be **byte-identical** to a fully-vsim baseline run for the same window (after `kanata_loop_skip` warmup).

### Phase 5 - Hardening

Each item independently scoped:
- **Vector regfile transfer**: measure DM cost; if >100ms per handoff, add mmap-shared V regfile via DPI (mirror the `set_ilm()` pattern in `src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:393-441`).
- **FP regs and `mstatus.FS`**: standard handling.
- **PMP**: all 64 pmpaddr/pmpcfg via Abstract Commands.
- **Priv-mode transitions**: set DCSR.prv so `dret` resumes in the right mode.
- **Traps inside ROI**: trap stays in vsim until exit-CSR retires.
- **MMIO touch in ROI**: add an interconnect watch (`src/platform/interconnect.hpp`), fail loudly on non-RAM addresses during the ROI window.
- **mcycle/minstret discontinuity**: sum across handoffs to keep monotonic; document the gap vs. fully-RTL.
- **lr/sc reservations**: explicitly invalidate via DCSR after resume.
- **Multi-hart**: ABI struct already carries per-hart arrays; `_NDS_NHART <= 2` already enforced (`src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:37-39`).

Test: torture workload running interrupt-driven code, V+FP active, traps inside ROI, verifying mcycle/minstret monotonic across multiple handoffs.

## 8. Phase 3 E2E Test Expansion

### 8.1 Context

Phase 3 landed the round-trip mechanism. The existing e2e harness `tests/hybrid/roundtrip_e2e.sh` exercises the full QEMU1 -> vsim -> QEMU2 path but with a single fixture (`handoff_roundtrip.S`) that only validates ONE GPR (`a2 == 3`) via the semihosting exit code. The plumbing works, but two large coverage gaps remain before we move on to Phase 4:

- **Register coverage**: 1 of 31 GPRs is observed; ABI-special regs (`sp`/`gp`/`tp`/`ra`/`fp`) are never touched; sign-bit and full-64-bit values are not exercised; the `HYBRID_HANDOFF_RUNTIME` trigger form (`csrrw rd,0x7C0,rs1`) is never fired in e2e.
- **Memory coverage**: zero tests today. Vsim never executes a load/store between the entry and exit triggers, so the integration of the cache/memory subsystem with the resumed state is unverified. Cross-simulator memory survival via shared mmap is also untested at e2e level (Phase 1 isolated unit test only).

### 8.2 What's actually transferable today (constraints on fixture design)

From `state_drain.hpp:28-45`, `resume_driver.hpp:46-68`, `qemu_handback.hpp:122-144`, `gdbstub_client.hpp:66-82`, and `hybrid_handoff.c:101-124`:

| State class | Fully round-tripped? |
|---|---|
| `x1..x31`, `pc` | yes |
| `priv` | hardcoded to 3 in both directions |
| `f0..f31`, all CSRs (mstatus/mepc/mtvec/...), V regs, PMP | NOT transferred (Phase 5) |
| Memory written in vsim, read in QEMU 2 | NOT transferred unless `--shared-mem-path` is wired into `qemu_handback.hpp` (currently it isn't) |

Implication: every Tier-1 fixture must encode pass/fail into a **GPR before the exit csrwi**, then in the QEMU 2 phase (after the csrwi, where DPC lands) verify and ship the result through `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED`. We cannot use FP/CSR/V state as test channels yet.

### 8.3 Fixture three-phase template

Every new fixture follows this layout (text base `0x80000000`, semihosting param block at `0x80001000` per the existing convention):

```
_start:                          // PHASE A: runs in QEMU 1 (functional)
    <set up initial state>
    csrwi   0x7C0, 0             // QEMU drains x1..x31+pc, exits

phase_b:                         // PHASE B: runs in vsim (RTL, cycle-accurate)
    <transform state under test>
    csrwi   0x7C0, 1             // vsim drains, exits 200
                                 //   on the way out, main spawns QEMU 2
                                 //   gdbstub restores x1..x31+pc, sends c
phase_c:                         // PHASE C: runs in QEMU 2 (functional)
    <fold result into checksum/bitmask in t2>
    li      t0, 0x80001000       // semihosting param block
    li      t1, 0x20026          // ADP_Stopped_ApplicationExit
    sd      t1, 0(t0)
    sd      t2, 8(t0)            // exit_code = checksum (0 == pass)
    li      a0, 0x20             // SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED
    mv      a1, t0
    .option push; .option norvc
    slli x0,x0,0x1f; ebreak; srai x0,x0,0x7
    .option pop
1:  j 1b
```

The single observable signal remains the QEMU 2 process exit code. Per fixture, the bash driver asserts that code matches an expected value (usually 0).

### 8.5 Tier-2 test (requires Phase 1 wiring into handback)

**`rt_shared_mem.sh`**: cross-simulator memory survival via mmap. This is the only test that detects regressions in "memory written in vsim is visible to QEMU 2" - the key promise of the hybrid architecture beyond Phase 3.

Required code change (small, but a real change beyond test scaffolding):
- `src/hybrid/qemu_handback.hpp:84-116` (`detail::spawn_qemu`) accepts an optional `shared_mem_path`. When set, `argv` is rebuilt to add `-object memory-backend-file,id=mem,share=on,mem-path=<path>,size=<size>` and `-machine virt,memory-backend=mem` (replacing `-M virt`).
- `src/main.cpp` plumbs the existing `--shared-mem-path` / `--shared-mem-size` flags into `run_qemu_handback`.
- Bash driver creates `/dev/shm/vsim-rt-shared` of the right size, launches QEMU 1 with `-object memory-backend-file,...,share=on`, vsim with `--shared-mem-path`, and asserts.

Fixture (`rt_shared_mem.S`):
- Phase A: write magic byte pattern (e.g., `0xA5` x 4096) to `0x8001_0000` from x10, csrwi entry.
- Phase B: vsim verifies pattern is intact (loop reading 4 KB into checksum), XORs page in place with `0x5A`, stores result of mask check to x10, csrwi exit.
- Phase C: QEMU 2 reads the same 4 KB through the shared mmap, recomputes checksum (should match `0xA5 ^ 0x5A` = `0xFF` everywhere), folds mismatch count into exit code.

Pass criterion: exit 0 AND wall-clock confirms two-sided visibility (page must be modified by both simulators).

> **NOTE**: Tier-2 is currently **blocked** by the memory-layout mismatch described in Section 9. The fixture, driver, Makefile entry, and CMake entry are already in tree; the actual e2e will only PASS after Section 9's redesign lands.

## 9. Cross-Simulator Shared Memory Redesign (Tier-2 Unblocking)

### 9.1 Context (why we are redesigning)

Section 8.5 promised a Tier-2 e2e that maps the same backing file across QEMU 1, vsim, and QEMU 2. The structural plumbing landed (`spawn_qemu` accepts `shared_mem_path`/`shared_mem_size`, `main.cpp` forwards both) but the test fails at vsim startup with:

```
[critical] fatal exception: SimpleMemory external_buffer smaller than region size
```

Root cause is a layout mismatch between vsim's "RAM is everything from 0" model and QEMU's "RAM starts at 0x80000000" model:

| | RAM region | data[0] = GPA | GPA 0x80010000 lands at file offset |
|---|---|---|---|
| vsim main_memory (today) | [0x0, 0x8FFFFFFF] = 2.25 GB (`memory_map.h:8-9`, `platform.hpp:74`) | 0x0 | 0x80010000 |
| QEMU virt machine | [0x80000000, 0x80000000+N) (`qemu_v5/hw/riscv/virt.c` VIRT_DRAM=0x80000000) | 0x80000000 | 0x10000 |

Two derived symptoms:
- `simple_memory.hpp:60-70` rejects external buffers smaller than the full 2.25 GB region, so any non-trivial mmap size fails before bytes are exchanged.
- `simulator.hpp:246` calls `read_elf_into_memory(*elf, xlen_t{0}, ...)` with a hardcoded base of 0; correctness depends on `region.base_address == 0`.

The intended outcome of this redesign: vsim and QEMU agree on which file byte backs which GPA, with no extra owned storage, and with the cycle-accurate path unchanged in non-hybrid mode.

### 9.2 Design (correctness first, then configurable)

**Principle**: in hybrid mode the mmap window IS the addressable RAM. The vsim `main_memory` region is narrowed at construction time to `[base_gpa, base_gpa + size)` where `base_gpa` matches QEMU virt's RAM base (0x80000000). No new "data origin within a wider region" abstraction; the region itself moves.

```
NON-HYBRID (preserved verbatim - default constructor path)
  Platform(ram_buffer = {})
    region        = [RAM_BASE = 0x0, RAM_END = 0x8FFFFFFF]   // 2.25 GB
    storage       = owned vector, 2.25 GB
    ELF load base = region.base_address = 0x0
    ELF segment at 0x80000000 -> data[0x80000000]            // works as today

HYBRID (--shared-mem-path P --shared-mem-size N [--shared-mem-base B = 0x80000000])
  MmapBacking mmap(P, N, base_gpa = B)
  Platform(ram_buffer = mmap.span(), ram_region = [B, B + N - 1])
    region        = [B, B + N - 1]                            // narrow window
    storage       = external mmap span, exactly N bytes       // size check passes
    ELF load base = region.base_address = B = 0x80000000
    ELF segment at 0x80000000 -> data[0]                      // file offset 0
    GPA 0x80010000 -> data[0x10000] = file offset 0x10000     // matches QEMU
    Alias region narrowed to {0,0} (degenerate -> contains() always false)
```

`--shared-mem-base` defaults to 0x80000000 so plain `--shared-mem-path X --shared-mem-size N` Just Works. The flag exists so that future QEMU machine types or alternate test harnesses can override without code changes — this is the "configurable" half of the requirement.

**Alternative considered and rejected**: keep the wide [0, 2.25 GB) region and add a `data_origin` field plus owned-fallback for non-mapped GPAs. Rejected because (a) hybrid mode never needs RAM outside the mmap window — anything below 0x80000000 in QEMU virt is MMIO/ROM, not RAM, and accessing it from vsim while purporting to share with QEMU is already undefined; (b) a sub-range data window doubles the SimpleMemory state machine (mapped vs owned per access); (c) it conflicts with the existing "external_buffer must cover the whole region" contract that other call sites rely on.

### 9.3 Code changes

**Modify**:

| File | Change | TDD phase |
|---|---|---|
| `src/platform/mmap_backing.hpp` | Add `uint64_t base_gpa_` field, constructor param `base_gpa = 0x80000000`, getter `base_gpa()`. Storage and span() unchanged. | structural |
| `src/platform/platform.hpp:71-75` | Platform constructor adds optional `MemoryRegion ram_region` (default `{RAM_BASE, RAM_END}`) and optional `MemoryRegion ram_alias_region` (default `{RAM_ALIAS_BASE, RAM_ALIAS_END}`). Used in main_memory construction. | structural |
| `src/simulator.hpp` (constructor, ~line 106) | Forward optional `ram_region` / `ram_alias_region` to Platform. | structural |
| `src/simulator.hpp:246` | `read_elf_into_memory(*elf, xlen_t{0}, ...)` -> `read_elf_into_memory(*elf, platform.main_memory.region.base_address, ...)`. | behavioral |
| `src/main.cpp` | New CLI flag `--shared-mem-base <hex>` (default 0x80000000). When `--shared-mem-path` is set, construct `MmapBacking(path, size, base_gpa)`, derive `ram_region = {base_gpa, base_gpa + size - 1}` and `ram_alias_region = {0, 0}`, pass to Simulator. | behavioral |

**Reuse without modification**:
- `src/platform/simple_memory.hpp` — translation `addr - region.base_address` already works for any region base; no change.
- `src/platform/memory_region.hpp` — `contains()` already exclusive-end aware; degenerate `{0,0}` (stored as [0,1)) contains nothing useful but never matches a real GPA.
- `src/utils/elf_loader.hpp` — already takes `base_addr` as a parameter; just gets a different value from the call site.
- `src/hybrid/qemu_handback.hpp` — `spawn_qemu` already builds `-object memory-backend-file,id=mem,share=on,mem-path=...,size=...` plus `-machine virt,memory-backend=mem`; no further change.
- `hybrid/test/rt_shared_mem.S`, `tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh`, `hybrid/test/Makefile`, `cmake/HybridConfig.cmake` — already in tree from prior session.

**Create**:
- `tests/hybrid/shared_mem_offset.test.cpp` — RED unit test for the redesigned path (see 9.4).

### 9.4 TDD sequence

Each step is one commit; commit messages start with `structural:` or `behavioral:` per `~/personal_git/.claude/TDD.md`.

**Step 1 — STRUCTURAL** (`structural: parameterize Platform RAM region for shared-mem mode`):
- Add `base_gpa_` to MmapBacking with default 0x80000000.
- Plumb optional `ram_region` / `ram_alias_region` through Simulator -> Platform.
- All defaults preserve existing behavior. `simulator.hpp:246` still uses hardcoded 0 in this commit.
- Run full `ctest`: every existing test passes (no behavioral change yet).

**Step 2 — RED** (`tests/hybrid/shared_mem_offset.test.cpp`):
- Allocate a temp 8 MB file (smaller than current 2.25 GB region; this alone proves the size-check breaks today).
- Construct Platform via `Simulator` with `ram_region = {0x80000000, 0x807FFFFF}`, `ram_buffer = mmap.span()`.
- Issue a SimpleMemory write via the SystemC TLM path at GPA 0x80010000 with a known 8-byte pattern.
- mmap the same file independently (read-only) and assert byte-at-file-offset 0x10000 equals the pattern.
- Test must FAIL on `main` (size-check) and FAIL after Step 1 alone (ELF loader not used in this unit test, but the size-check will already pass after Step 1; the assert that 0x80010000 lands at file offset 0x10000 is what genuinely fails until Step 3).

**Step 3 — GREEN / BEHAVIORAL** (`behavioral: load ELF at region.base_address; honor shared-mem-base in CLI`):
- Change `simulator.hpp:246` to use `platform.main_memory.region.base_address`.
- Add `--shared-mem-base` CLI flag in `main.cpp`, derive `ram_region` from MmapBacking when shared mode is active, narrow alias region to `{0,0}`.
- Step 2's unit test now passes.
- Run `bash tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh`: Phase A fills 4 KB, Phase B verifies + XORs to 0xFF, Phase C confirms via shared mmap, exit 0.

**Step 4 — REGRESSION**:
- `ctest -L hybrid-e2e --output-on-failure`: 8 existing Tier-1 round-trip fixtures still PASS (they don't use `--shared-mem-path`, so they exercise the unchanged non-hybrid path); `test_e2e_rt_shared_mem` now PASSES.
- Full non-hybrid `ctest`: bare-metal tests linked at low addresses (`spin.ld` at 0x80, `handoff.ld` at 0x10000) still load and run because the default RAM region remains [0x0, 0x90000000).
- `tests/hybrid/memory_map.test.cpp`: passes (constants in `memory_map.h` unchanged).

### 9.5 Critical files

| File | Action |
|---|---|
| `src/platform/mmap_backing.hpp` | modify (add base_gpa) |
| `src/platform/platform.hpp` | modify (constructor accepts optional ram_region) |
| `src/simulator.hpp` | modify (forward ram_region; use region.base_address as ELF load base) |
| `src/main.cpp` | modify (--shared-mem-base flag; derive ram_region in hybrid mode) |
| `tests/hybrid/shared_mem_offset.test.cpp` | create (RED then GREEN unit test) |
| `cmake/HybridConfig.cmake` | already wires `test_e2e_rt_shared_mem`; no change |
| `tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh`, `hybrid/test/rt_shared_mem.S`, `hybrid/test/Makefile` | already in tree; no change |

### 9.6 Verification

End-to-end ladder (each step requires the previous):

1. **Unit (RED -> GREEN)**: `tests/hybrid/shared_mem_offset.test.cpp` — write at GPA 0x80010000 via SimpleMemory, observe at file offset 0x10000 via independent mmap.
2. **Tier-2 e2e**: `ctest -R test_e2e_rt_shared_mem` — round-trip exits 0; QEMU 1 fills 4 KB with 0xA5..., vsim verifies + XORs to 0xFF..., QEMU 2 confirms.
3. **Tier-1 regression**: `ctest -L hybrid-e2e` — all 8 existing rt_* fixtures still PASS.
4. **Non-hybrid regression**: full `ctest` (no `--shared-mem-path`) — `spin`, `handoff`, `handoff_exit`, kanata, pipeview, all unit tests pass.
5. **Diagnostic**: deliberately set `--shared-mem-base 0x90000000` (wrong); rt_shared_mem.sh fails with a clear "ELF segment at 0x80000000 outside region" error rather than silent corruption.

### 9.7 Out of scope (documented, not blocked-on)

- The 64-bit alias region [0x800000000, 0x880000000): QEMU virt has no alias mirror. In hybrid mode the alias is degenerate; any access to it is undefined behavior, consistent with the existing "MMIO inside ROI" rule in Section 5 / Phase 5.
- Sharing ILM/DLM across simulators: separate regions, separate mmap, separate plan.
- Alternative QEMU machine types with non-0x80000000 RAM bases: enabled by `--shared-mem-base` flag but not exercised by Tier-2.
- mcycle/minstret continuity across the handoff: already documented as a known discontinuity in Section 5.

4	workflow. End-to-end pipeline:
5	
6	1. **BBV generation** - run the workload in QEMU; emit Basic Block Vectors
7	   (one per fixed-size slice of dynamic instructions) into a `.bb` file.
8	2. **SimPoint clustering** - feed the `.bb` file into the SimPoint tool; it
9	   selects K representative slices and weights.
10	3. **Checkpoint collection** - replay the workload in QEMU, drain a checkpoint
11	   (architectural state + memory image) at each of the K simpoint boundaries.
12	4. **Detailed replay** - for each checkpoint, restore into vsim/Verilator and
13	   collect cycle-accurate metrics. Combine per-region metrics by weight.
14

16	repeated K times at SimPoint-selected boundaries instead of at WFI / CSR write.
17	The transport choice for that handoff matters: per-region snapshot cost
18	multiplies by K (often 10-30), and the trigger surface must accept "fire at
19	instruction count N", not just "fire at this PC".

22

# SimPoint-driven hybrid sim: shared-mmap transport vs dump/reload transport

Context: extend the existing QEMU + vsim/Verilator hybrid with a SimPoint-driven
workflow. End-to-end pipeline:

1. **BBV generation** - run the workload in QEMU; emit Basic Block Vectors
   (one per fixed-size slice of dynamic instructions) into a `.bb` file.
2. **SimPoint clustering** - feed the `.bb` file into the SimPoint tool; it
   selects K representative slices and weights.
3. **Checkpoint collection** - replay the workload in QEMU, drain a checkpoint
   (architectural state + memory image) at each of the K simpoint boundaries.
4. **Detailed replay** - for each checkpoint, restore into vsim/Verilator and
   collect cycle-accurate metrics. Combine per-region metrics by weight.

Steps 3 and 4 are exactly the QEMU -> vsim handoff we already have, just
repeated K times at SimPoint-selected boundaries instead of at WFI / CSR write.
The transport choice for that handoff matters: per-region snapshot cost
multiplies by K (often 10-30), and the trigger surface must accept "fire at
instruction count N", not just "fire at this PC".

Two candidate transports exist in our tree today:

1. The **shared-mmap** transport used by `hybrid_sim/` (this repo).
2. The **dump/reload** transport used by `/local/nick/vsim-demo-new/demo/hybrid`.

This document compares them and recommends a path.

---

## 1. Mechanics side-by-side

| Aspect | `hybrid_sim` (shared mmap) | `vsim-demo-new/demo/hybrid` (dump/reload) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory transport | Shared `/dev/shm` file mmap'd by QEMU1, vsim, QEMU2 via `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,...`. Zero-copy. | `dump-guest-memory` -> ELF core -> `tools/qemu_elfdump_to_main_memory.sh` extracts PT_LOAD 0 -> gzip -> vsim `--restore-memory <gz>`. |
| Arch state format | Binary `hybrid_state_v1` struct in `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`. | Drain mechanism | QEMU TCG plugin `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` triggered on `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` or on ELF-symbol PC. Programmatic, no human in the loop. | Manual rendezvous on WFI: `Ctrl-A c` for `dump-guest-memory`, then attach GDB and `source tools/dump_ae350.gdb`. |
| Restore mechanism | vsim `ResumeDriver` (DMI Abstract Commands + Program Buffer); for QEMU2, `qemu_handback` spawns QEMU2 and pushes registers via gdbstub P-packets. | `./sim --restore-memory snap --reset-vector PC`, then attach GDB and `source ae350regs.cmds`, `set $dpc`, `continue`. |
| V extension support | Yes. V regs go through scratch GPA `0x80FF0000` via `vse8.v`; vtype/vl restored via Program Buffer `vsetvl`; vstart/vxsat/vxrm/vcsr via Abstract Cmds. | Not supported. `dump_ae350.gdb` lists only Andes V5 CSRs - no V regs and no V CSRs. |
| Trigger surface | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0/1` (default) or ELF-symbol PC (`_hybrid_enter_pc`/`_hybrid_exit_pc`). Both extensible from the TCG plugin (insn-count, region marker, ...). | WFI only; manual UI driving. Extending requires GDB scripting or QMP scripting. |
| Per-handoff latency | Microseconds: mmap is shared (no copy); state file is a few KB binary. | Seconds: 2 GiB gzip pass + ELF parsing + GDB scripting per drain and per restore. |
| Coupling between sides | Tight - both sides include the same C ABI header. | Loose - the GDB remote protocol is the only contract. |
| Auditability of snapshot | Binary; needs a viewer (or a converter to text). | Human-readable `ae350regs.txt` is the wire format. Easy to diff. |

- `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp` (drain on vsim side)

1 # How the Hybrid Simulator Works 2 3 A friendly tour for engineers new to this codebase. 4 5 ## The Problem in One Paragraph 6 7 A pure RTL simulator (`vsim`, built on Verilator + SystemC) is **cycle-accurate**: it tells you exactly how many cycles every instruction takes inside the CPU pipeline. The price is speed: it runs at ~5-9 KHz. Booting Linux, running libc init, and warming up before reaching the actual code you care about can take hours. Meanwhile, QEMU runs the same program **functionally** at MHz speed, but it has no notion of pipeline cycles. 8 9 The hybrid simulator combines them: run the boring parts in QEMU, switch to vsim for the small region of interest (ROI) you actually want to measure, and (optionally) hand back to QEMU to finish. 10 11 ## High-Level Concept 12 13 Five big ideas make this possible: 14 15 1. **Memory is shared, not copied.** Both simulators `mmap` the same backing file (`/dev/shm/...`) as their RAM. When QEMU writes a byte at `0x80001000`, vsim sees it instantly, and vice versa. RAM size becomes irrelevant. 16 17 2. **All architectural state fits in one packed struct.** GPRs, FPRs, M-mode CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mtvec, ...), PMP (16 cfg + 64 addr), V scalar CSRs (vtype, vl, vstart, vxsat, vxrm, vcsr, vlenb), and the full V regfile (`v0..v31`, up to 2 KB) all live in `struct hybrid_state_v1` (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`). That struct is the wire contract every component agrees on. 18 19 3. **vsim only refreshes what the ROI can mutate.** vsim restores into the hart only what it needs to run (GPRs + PC + V state); on the way out it drains back only what could have changed (GPRs + PC + V state). Everything else (FP, M-mode CSRs, PMP, V regfile bytes when the ROI doesn't touch them) **passes through unchanged** from the QEMU 1 snapshot to the QEMU 2 restore. This skips ~85+ DMI transactions per handoff. 20 21 4. **The trigger is a magic CSR write or a PC match.** `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` enters vsim; `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` exits it. CSR `0x7C0` is in the user-defined range. A third form, `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1`, decides at runtime based on `rs1`'s value (1 = exit). PC-trigger mode skips the magic CSR entirely - the orchestrator names entry/exit PCs on the CLI. 22 23 5. **Microarchitectural state is thrown away.** Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer - none of it transfers. We accept cold caches at handoff and rely on a "warm-up window" before recording. 24 25 ## State Categories and Their Transport 26 27 The wire struct is uniform; transport per category depends on what QEMU's plugin API and the AX45MPV Debug Module are willing to expose. 28 29 | Category | QEMU 1 -> snapshot | snapshot -> vsim hart | vsim hart -> snapshot | snapshot -> QEMU 2 | 30 |---------------------------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|--------------------| 31 | GPRs `x1..x31`, PC | TCG plugin reads | DM Abstract Commands (DPC for PC) | DM Abstract Commands (fresh read) | gdbstub `G` packet | 32 | `mtvec` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Command | passthrough (not re-read) | gdbstub `P` | 33 | Other M-mode CSRs (`mstatus`, `mepc`, `mscratch`, `medeleg`, `mideleg`, `mip`, `mie`, `mcycle`, `minstret`, `mcause`, `mtval`, `satp`) | TCG plugin | passthrough (not written) | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (per `kCsrByName`) | 34 | FPRs `f0..f31` | TCG plugin | passthrough | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (per `hybrid_fpr_names`) | 35 | PMP (16 cfg + 64 addr) | TCG plugin | passthrough | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (each slot) | 36 | V scalar CSRs - `vtype`, `vl` | TCG plugin | DM Program Buffer running `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` | DM Abstract Command (fresh read) | gdbstub `P` | 37 | V scalar CSRs - `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, `mstatus.VS` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Command, **with Program-Buffer fallback** if the DM rejects | DM Abstract Command (fresh read) | gdbstub `P` | 38 | V regfile `v0..v31` (up to 2 KB) | TCG plugin | shared-mmap scratch region (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`); fixture executes `vle8.v` to load | fixture executes `vse8.v` back to scratch; vsim memcpys those bytes into `state.vreg[]` | gdbstub `P` (per V-reg index) | 39 | `vlenb` | TCG plugin | not restored (read-only, fixed by NDS_VLEN) | passthrough (verified, not re-read) | not sent | 40 | Guest memory | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | 41 42 "Passthrough" means: vsim copies the field from the inbound snapshot into the outbound snapshot unchanged. The hart never sees it; the field rides from QEMU 1 directly to QEMU 2. 43 44 ## Trigger Modes 45 46 Two ways to delimit the ROI; pick at runtime via `TRIGGER_MODE=csr|pc`. 47 48 | Mode | Entry into vsim | Exit out of vsim | Source of truth | 49 |------|------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------| 50 | CSR | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` (or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with `rs1==0`) | `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` (or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with `rs1==1`) | the binary itself | 51 | PC | plugin arg `enter_pc=0x...` (no insn match) | vsim CLI `--hybrid-exit-pc 0x...` (synthetic hit added via `CsrTrigger::add_pc_hit`) | the orchestrator/CLI; binary is unchanged | 52 53 Three handoff kinds (`hybrid_handoff_kind` in `include/hybrid/insn_match.h`): 54 55 - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_ENTER` - QEMU drains and exits; vsim ignores at retire. 56 - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_EXIT` - vsim drains and exits; QEMU does not encounter this PC. 57 - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_RUNTIME` - vsim decodes `rs1` from the matched insn, reads its current GPR value, fires only when it equals 1. Lets a single binary parameterize entry vs exit at runtime. 58 59 ## Design Rationale 60 61 **One packed struct is the wire format.** Three components in three languages (QEMU plugin in C, vsim in C++, gdbstub-translation layer in C++) need to agree byte-for-byte on what state means. A C struct in a shared header (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`) is the simplest contract that compiles into all three. Versioning is via `magic` + `version`, with `reserved` bytes for forward growth (vxsat/vxrm/vcsr were added by consuming `reserved` rather than bumping to v2). 62 63 **QEMU 1 -> vsim is file-based, vsim -> QEMU 2 is gdbstub-based.** QEMU mainline is used **as-is** (no patches, no fork). It exposes two relevant extension points: the TCG plugin API can *read* state during execution, and gdbstub `-S` can *write* state before the first fetch. Plugins cannot inject state at startup, and there is no `--load-state-from-file` flag. Each direction picks the only working primitive: the plugin drains to a file out of QEMU 1, and gdbstub `G`/`P` packets seed QEMU 2. The vsim side translates between the file and the RSP packets - QEMU never has to know about `hybrid_state_v1`. 64 65 **Passthrough is the largest performance lever.** A naive design would round-trip every field of `hybrid_state_v1` through DM Abstract Commands - hundreds of DMI transactions. Instead, `HandoffController::set_passthrough_input(snapshot)` keeps QEMU 1's drained state in vsim memory; when the exit trigger fires, `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough(snapshot)` re-reads only x1..x31 + PC + (when V) the six V CSRs via DM, and merges those over the snapshot. Everything the ROI is *not allowed* to touch (FPRs, M-mode CSRs except mtvec, PMP) flows from QEMU 1 straight into the QEMU 2 gdbstub restore. ROI-mutates-FP would silently lose those changes - that's an explicit, documented limitation, not an oversight. 66 67 **The Debug Module handles most ISA registers; `vtype`/`vl` need the Program Buffer.** GPRs, FPRs, and ordinary CSRs all have abstract-command slots in the AX45MPV DM - one DMI transaction each. `vtype` is the exception: the V spec marks it read-only via direct CSR write because writing it sets the `vill` flag and invalidates the vector unit. The only legal restore for `vtype` + `vl` together is the `vsetvl` instruction, which has to *execute on the hart*. The Program Buffer is exactly that slot: stage `vl` and `vtype` in scratch GPRs (x5/x6), then execute `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` in the PB. The GPR loop afterwards overwrites x5/x6, so no leakage. 68 69 **Tier-A V CSRs use Abstract-first with a Program-Buffer fallback.** `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, and `mstatus.VS` are spec-compliant to direct CSR write, but the AX45MPV DM may reject the abstract path on a given build. `ResumeDriver::write_csr_abstract_or_pb` tries Abstract first, checks `abstractcs.cmderr`, and on failure clears the sticky error and falls back to a one-instruction PB sequence (`csrrw x0, csr, x5`). The fallback is silent and per-CSR, so a partially-implemented DM does not block the rest of the restore. 70 71 **The V regfile rides shared mmap, not the Debug Module.** The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command slots for `v0..v31`; it leaves V to optional Program Buffer or DPI extensions, and the AX45MPV wrapper does not implement either. Pushing 32 x 64-byte regs through the Program Buffer one element at a time would be both slow and intricate. Since both simulators already share an mmap, we reuse it: vsim copies `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region before `dret`, and the fixture's vsim-phase prologue issues `vle8.v` from that region to load the real V regfile. Drain mirrors: the fixture's epilogue issues `vse8.v` to scratch, and the simulator wrapper reads those bytes back into `state.vreg[]` after `StateDrain` returns. The fixture is aware of the contract; the V regfile reuses the mmap that already exists for general memory. 72 73 **Missing-register tolerance is part of the contract.** A given QEMU build may not advertise every PMP slot, and the DM may not implement every CSR. `apply_state` in `qemu_handback.hpp` silently skips any name not in the discovered RegMap (`try_write_named`); `StateDrain::read_64bit_register` returns 0 and clears `cmderr` when the DM rejects. Failing closed would block bring-up on every QEMU/RTL revision; failing soft keeps the happy path moving and surfaces real bugs at the fixture level. 74 75 ## Architecture Flowchart 76 77 ```mermaid 78 flowchart LR 79 subgraph DISK["Shared backing files"] 80 SHM[("/dev/shm RAM
(mmap shared)
+ V scratch
at 0x80FF0000")]

The hybrid simulator combines them: run the boring parts in QEMU, switch to vsim for the small region of interest (ROI) you actually want to measure, and (optionally) hand back to QEMU to finish. ## High-Level Concept Five big ideas make this possible: 1. **Memory is shared, not copied.** Both simulators `mmap` the same backing file (`/dev/shm/...`) as their RAM. When QEMU writes a byte at `0x80001000`, vsim sees it instantly, and vice versa. # Hybrid Simulator: gem5 CPU-switch parity

> Status: Draft | Author: Nick Hung | Date: 2026-05-16 | Reviewers: self (design archive for own simulator)

## 1. 摘要與目標 (Summary & Objective)

Extend the QEMU + vsim hybrid simulator to support all four CPU-switch mechanisms described in `specs/gem5-cpu-model-switch.md` (instruction-count marker, phase-boundary signal, checkpoint completion, guest-driven request) on a single ISA (RV64). Today only the guest-driven path is wired, and the pipeline is forced linear (QEMU1 -> vsim -> QEMU2). The output is a design archive that records what shape of redesign keeps QEMU as a mainline binary, keeps vsim untouched on the cycle-accurate path, and makes new trigger sources additive rather than disruptive.

## 2. 背景與痛點 (Background & Problem Statement)

The hybrid simulator runs a workload in QEMU (functional, MHz) until the user hits a trigger, drains architectural state, replays the same state into vsim (cycle-accurate, ~5-9 KHz), runs the region of interest, drains again, and hands back to a second QEMU. This is one of gem5's four switch shapes: guest-driven request via `csrwi 0x7C0, 0/1`. Three other shapes are missing:

- **Instruction-count switch.** Workloads that do not embed the magic CSR (third-party binaries, legacy ELFs, JIT-generated code) cannot be cut at a precise dynamic-instruction offset. The TCG plugin sees every retired instruction but has no counter-trigger today. vsim's retire signal taps fire per commit but no per-icount drain hook exists either.
- **Phase-boundary switch.** SimPoint-style sampling needs the simulator to fire on workload-phase entries, not on PC matches embedded by hand. BBV emission and the trigger that consumes the cluster file do not exist. `docs/simpoint_transport_comparison.md` already recommends the shared-mmap transport for this; the trigger surface itself is still missing.
- **Checkpoint replay.** A snapshot taken under QEMU cannot be replayed independently of the run that produced it. Today the snapshot is a transient `state.bin` consumed by the next process in the pipeline; there is no archive format, no metadata, and no entry point that says "start vsim from this archived snapshot".

A second mismatch is orthogonal: the current pipeline is **linear and one-shot**. There is no way to switch QEMU -> vsim -> QEMU -> vsim ... N times in a single run. SimPoint with K=10 regions needs K independent (snapshot, replay) pairs and today's orchestrator (a shell script that wires three exec calls) cannot express it.

The compound effect: every workflow that wants more than "one fast prefix and one slow ROI" - phase sampling, multi-region SimPoint, retroactive analysis of an archived snapshot, flip-flop comparison - hits the same shortfall. Adding each capability as an isolated patch creates a different control-flow shape per use case. Recording the unified design now is cheaper than discovering it three projects from now.

## 3. 架構設計與核心邏輯 (Architecture & Core Logic)

### 3.1 The three roles (mapped onto QEMU+vsim)

```
                +--------------------------------+
                |   policy layer                 |
                |   (decides WHEN to switch)     |
                |                                |
                |   trigger sources:             |
                |   - CSR write       (today)    |
                |   - ELF symbol PC   (today)    |
                |   - dynamic icount  (new)      |
                |   - BBV phase index (new)      |
                |   - checkpoint load (new)      |
                +-------------+------------------+
                              |
                              v
                +--------------------------------+
                |   switch orchestrator          |
                |   (executes the swap protocol) |
                |                                |
                |   today: run_e2e.sh +          |
                |          qemu_handback (split) |
                |   new:   single session driver |
                |          owns mmap fd + plan   |
                +-------------+------------------+
                              |
              +---------------+---------------+
              v                               v
        +------------+                  +------------+
        |  QEMU      |  --- handoff --->|  vsim      |
        | (process)  |                  | (process)  |
        +-----+------+                  +------+-----+
              |                                |
              +-------------+------------------+
                            v
        +----------------------------------------+
        | shared simulated machine               |
        |   /dev/shm guest RAM (mmap)            |
        |   V scratch at GPA 0x80FF0000          |
        |   hybrid_state_v1 file (per handoff)   |
        +----------------------------------------+
```

Three roles, three layers. Today the orchestrator is fused with the policy layer inside the plugin (the plugin both decides "the CSR fired" and "drain now"). The redesign separates them: the policy layer produces *signals*, the orchestrator decides *what happens next*. That separation is the precondition for adding new triggers without forking the spawn path.

### 3.2 The "co-existence" decision, mapped to subprocesses

gem5 keeps both CPU instances live and parks the dormant one in-place. We cannot do that here: an idle vsim process holds GB-scale Verilator state plus a SystemC kernel; keeping it warm across a 10-minute QEMU prefix is wasteful, and multiplying that by N coexisting variants is worse.

The hybrid sim's substitute is **persistent shared state + ephemeral processes**:

- The **simulated machine** lives outside any CPU process: it is the shared mmap (guest RAM + V scratch region). It is born when the orchestrator creates the backing file and persists for the whole session.
- A **CPU process** (QEMU or vsim) is spawned when needed, runs until its trigger fires, drains to a state file, and exits. The orchestrator inherits its exit code and the populated state file.
- "Active vs dormant" maps to "running vs not-yet-spawned (or exited)". Switching from one model to the other is: exit the current process, spawn the next one, hand it the state file plus the mmap.

Corollaries (the same shape as gem5's, with the substitution):

- A region is born with a chosen first model; the orchestrator picks up front, not the binary.
- Switching back is the same operation: exit the current process, spawn the other model, pass it the same state file format. Direction is not baked in.
- N-way switching is "N spawns" with one mmap that outlives all of them. No new mechanism beyond persistent mmap + serial spawn.

This converts "can the dormant CPU survive while the active one runs?" into "can the mmap survive between two consecutive spawns?". The latter is trivially yes - it is a `/dev/shm` file held by an fd open in the orchestrator. The trade-off we pay vs gem5: a 1-2 s spawn cost per switch instead of a function call. Against a 100-second cycle-accurate window, that is rounding error; against a 1-minute SimPoint replay batch of 10 regions, it is 10-20 s of overhead total.

### 3.3 What is preserved vs. what is replaced

| Class | Where it lives | Survives switch? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Guest RAM | `/dev/shm` mmap | Yes (zero-copy) | mmap fd held by orchestrator across spawns |
| Architectural regs / PC / CSRs | `hybrid_state_v1` file | Yes (file is the contract) | Per-thread; ABI-locked |
| V regfile | `state.vreg[]` + V scratch GPA `0x80FF0000` | Yes (file + scratch) | Fixture-aware contract: vsim writes scratch, fixture issues `vle8.v` |
| FPRs, M-mode CSRs, PMP | `hybrid_state_v1` file | Yes (passthrough) | vsim does not re-read these; they ride QEMU1 -> QEMU2 unchanged |
| QEMU TCG TLB / softmmu cache | QEMU process memory | No | New QEMU spawn starts cold |
| vsim BPU / caches / TLB / store buffer | vsim process memory | No | New vsim spawn starts cold; kanata-loop-skip warms |

Microarchitectural state is intentionally discarded at every switch boundary, consistent with gem5's "TLB is CPU-private" rule. The kanata-loop-skip mechanism (already in vsim) is the documented warmup window before the recorded slice. The deliberate cost of the persistent-state-plus-ephemeral-process model is repeated cold start per ROI; the deliberate benefit is no in-process CPU teardown logic.

### 3.4 The swap protocol (the shape already exists)

The three-phase shape is implemented today; making it explicit lets new triggers slot in without case-by-case changes.

1. **Quiesce.** The CPU process commits its in-flight work and returns to a documented "at rest" point. For QEMU, this is `qemu_plugin_request_exit()` after the plugin has read every register. For vsim, this is `StateDrain` after a DMI halt and `dret`. No timed events outlive the process exit; no architectural fact survives without being either in the state file or in the mmap.
2. **Hand off.** The orchestrator (a) collects the exited process's state file, (b) optionally rewrites it (checkpoint case: load from archive instead of from the just-exited child), (c) decides the next model. The mmap is reused; it is not closed or copied. The state file is the only architectural artifact that crosses the boundary.
3. **Resume.** The orchestrator spawns the next process with the state file as input. QEMU consumes the file via gdbstub `G`/`P` packets before `continue`; vsim consumes it via `ResumeDriver` (DM Abstract Commands + Program Buffer + V scratch). Execution continues from the same PC; the simulated clock does not advance.

The protocol is uniform regardless of trigger kind. A new trigger source adds a *reason* the process exits, not a new exit path.

### 3.5 Why a universal quiescence contract (already true, almost by accident)

Both processes exit cleanly at switch time. The TCG plugin's `request_exit` flushes QEMU's TB cache and returns through the main loop. vsim's `StateDrain` halts the hart via DMI and returns architectural state without leaving Verilator in an intermediate state. No bespoke per-trigger drain logic exists or is needed.

What changes with new triggers: the *cause* of the quiesce is different (icount overflow vs CSR write vs phase-index match vs orchestrator-injected exit signal). The drain path is the same. The orchestrator polls "did the process exit cleanly with code 200?" and reads the state file. A new trigger ships as "an additional reason to set `should_exit = true` inside the plugin"; nothing else moves.

### 3.6 Trigger sources (the deliverable)

Four trigger sources, each implementable independently against the same orchestrator contract:

- **Guest-driven request** (today). `csrwi 0x7C0, 0/1` or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with the runtime kind. Both QEMU plugin and vsim's `CsrTrigger` already implement; the ELF can also embed an `_hybrid_enter_pc`/`_hybrid_exit_pc` symbol for PC-mode. No change.

- **Instruction-count marker** (additive). Both QEMU and vsim gain a per-insn counter and a `--switch-at-icount N` argument. QEMU's TCG plugin already sees every retired insn; the counter is a single uint64 incremented in the per-insn callback. vsim's retire signal tap (`common_signals_45.hpp`) already fires per commit; a sibling trigger class IcountTrigger sits alongside the existing CsrTrigger and votes for drain at commit time. Wire format unchanged.

- **Phase-boundary signal** (BBV-driven). The TCG plugin emits BBVs every fixed slice (e.g., 100M dynamic insns) into a `.bb` file during a profiling pass. An offline SimPoint clustering pass selects K representative slice indices and weights. A second plugin run consumes the slice-index file and fires the drain trigger when the dynamic insn counter reaches each chosen slice. Mechanically this is the icount trigger with a slice-aware policy on top - the same `should_exit` mechanism, a different policy that decides when to set it. SimPoint clustering is offline and out of scope for the plugin.

- **Checkpoint completion** (orchestrator-side). The orchestrator persists, per switch boundary, both the `state.bin` and a snapshot of the mmap backing file into `snap/<N>/{state.bin, mem}`. To replay a checkpoint, the orchestrator points a new mmap at `snap/<N>/mem`, copies `snap/<N>/state.bin` into the working state-file path, and spawns vsim (or QEMU) from there. No plugin or vsim change is needed; the orchestrator gains a `replay-checkpoint <dir>` entry point and a snapshot policy.

All four reduce to the same downstream: "a process exits cleanly with a populated state file; the mmap holds the rest". The orchestrator does not need to know *why* the process exited, only that it did, and what to do next per the session plan.

### 3.7 The orchestrator (the missing piece)

Today the orchestrator is split: `run_e2e.sh` for tests, `qemu_handback.hpp` for spawning QEMU2, plugin args + CLI flags for direction. There is no single entry point that owns "run a session of N switches".

The redesign adds one program (script or small C++ binary) that:

- Owns the mmap fd for the whole session and passes it (or its path) to each spawn.
- Holds the trigger plan: an ordered list of `(model, trigger_spec)` pairs that says, in effect, "first run QEMU until icount=5e9, then vsim until next CSR exit, then QEMU until end-of-program; on each boundary, persist a checkpoint into snap/N/".
- Spawns one process at a time per the plan; after each exit, reads the state file, optionally archives it, and consults the plan for the next action.
- Surfaces a `replay-checkpoint` mode that pre-populates the mmap and the state file from an archived directory before spawning, instead of starting from the original ELF.

The orchestrator is the only place that needs to know about all four trigger sources. The plugin and vsim see only the kind that applies to them per spawn.

## 4. 介面與資料合約 (Interface & Data Contract)

Four contracts, three of which already exist or are nearly there. The fourth (orchestrator -> CPU) is what the redesign formalizes.

- **CPU model contract.** Each CPU process accepts an input state (a state file plus a shared-mmap path), runs until its drain trigger fires, writes a state file on the way out, and exits with a documented code (0 = workload completed, 200 = drain-and-handoff, non-zero non-200 = failure). vsim already does this; QEMU does it through the plugin's `request_exit`. Each model owns its in-process trigger registration (TCG per-insn callbacks; vsim's CsrTrigger / future IcountTrigger). A model that cannot participate (e.g., a KVM-accelerated QEMU, where the plugin cannot intercept) opts out by failing the orchestrator's pre-spawn check before any side effect occurs.

- **Per-thread architectural state contract.** `hybrid_state_v1` (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`), already byte-locked by `state_abi_check.h`. The redesign does not change the wire format; new triggers do not need new fields. Multi-hart is structurally represented (`hart_id`) though only single-hart workloads are exercised today. The struct's `flags` already gate which state blocks are live; future trigger metadata, if needed at all, lands in the `reserved` window.

- **Shared-machine contract.** Guest RAM lives in one `/dev/shm` file backed by `memory-backend-file,share=on` on the QEMU side and by `mmap_backing.hpp` on the vsim side. The orchestrator owns the fd; spawned processes inherit it by path. V regfile transport uses a fixed GPA (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`) inside the same mmap. The contract is "every byte at GPA X reads identically on both sides at boundary moments"; vsim's `simple_memory.hpp` redesign (hybrid_plan.md Section 9) made that true by aligning vsim's RAM region with QEMU virt's RAM base.

- **Orchestrator -> CPU contract** (new). A spawned process is invoked with a uniform set of args:
  - `--state-in <path>` (read at startup) or `--cold-start` for the session's first QEMU.
  - `--state-out <path>` (written on drain).
  - `--mem-path <path>` (mmap; shared across spawns; size derived from the file).
  - `--trigger-spec <opaque>` - per-trigger-kind arguments. CSR is the default; PC/icount/phase pass enough to identify the firing condition (the specific PC, the icount target, the slice-index list).

  The CPU does not know about other CPUs or other switches in the session. The orchestrator owns sequencing.

Under the subprocess model, gem5's "connection rebinding" and "quiescence" contracts collapse into the orchestrator-CPU contract: rebinding is "the orchestrator passes the same mmap path to the next spawn"; quiescence is "the process exited with code 200 and the state file passes magic+version checks". The load-bearing boundary is this new one; once it stabilizes, adding a trigger is "extend the trigger-spec grammar plus add a callback to the plugin or to vsim".

## 5. 邊界條件與例外處理 (Edge Cases & Error Handling)

| Scenario | Likelihood | Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two trigger conditions fire on the same retired insn (e.g., icount + CSR) | Medium | Plugin / vsim resolves with a fixed priority: CSR/PC > phase > icount; only the highest-priority is honored | Multi-fire on the same boundary doubles state files; one rule, all triggers |
| Plugin or vsim exits without writing a complete state file | Low | Orchestrator validates magic, version, and exact size before acting; mismatched -> session fails immediately | Silent truncation is the worst-case bug; visible failure here is cheaper than a corrupt replay |
| Checkpoint replay against a mmap whose backing file is missing or has the wrong size | Medium (archived sessions) | Orchestrator verifies file presence and size against archived metadata before any spawn | Checkpoint archives are the long-lived artifact; broken archives must be obvious at load time, not at retire time |
| Snapshot taken on one CPU variant, replayed on another | Low | ABI struct's `flags` identifies live state classes; orchestrator rejects when target variant cannot consume them (e.g., `HYBRID_FLAG_V` set, target lacks V) | The wire format does not know what CPU is consuming it; the orchestrator does |
| Phase-trigger fires while QEMU is mid-TB | Medium (BBV) | Plugin always defers the drain to the next retired-insn boundary; never preempts TB-internal | Consistent with how CSR/PC triggers already work; one rule, all triggers |
| N-way switch with V-state and stale V scratch from a previous spawn | Low | Each spawn writes V scratch from its `state.vreg[]` before the fixture issues any `vle8.v`; previous bytes are overwritten | Scratch is single-writer per spawn; orchestrator does not need to clear it |
| Stale `state.bin` from a previous session sharing the same /tmp path | Low | Orchestrator writes all per-session files into a session-scoped `mktemp -d`; no path is reused across sessions | Path collisions across runs would silently feed bad data |
| Trigger fires on a hart that does not exist (`hart_id` out of range) | Low | Orchestrator rejects at plan-validation time, before the first spawn | Cheap to check up front; expensive to debug if missed mid-session |
| KVM/HVF-accelerated QEMU in the spawn list | Out of scope | Orchestrator refuses such a spawn at plan-validation; document the requirement | Accel modes bypass TCG and the plugin API; no drain primitive exists |
| MMIO touched inside a vsim ROI | Out of scope | Existing interconnect-watch (Phase 5 of hybrid_plan.md) fails loudly | Device state has no mirror in RTL; orthogonal to switch mechanism |
| Cross-ISA switch (e.g., RV64 -> RV32) | Out of scope | ABI struct is RV64-only; no translation contract exists | Different design problem; not in this spec |

The two failure modes that bit us during Phase-3 e2e (truncated state file, mmap size mismatch) are already in the test matrix (`rt_corrupt_state.sh`, the `simple_memory.hpp` size-check landed by hybrid_plan.md Section 9). New triggers do not introduce new failure modes; they reuse the existing quiesce/spawn pair.

## 6. 替代方案與權衡 (Alternatives Considered & Trade-offs)

### Alternative A: Persistent-process orchestrator (gem5-style "both alive")

- Approach: never exit either CPU process. Keep QEMU and vsim both running in paused states; switching becomes "tell one to drain, tell the other to resume" over IPC (Unix socket, shared memory queue).
- Pros: avoids the 1-2 s spawn cost per switch; closer to gem5's literal "both CPUs co-resident" model; trivial flip-flop sampling between two paused models.
- Cons: a paused vsim still holds GB of Verilator state; a paused QEMU still holds its TB cache and softmmu translations. Memory cost is multiplied by however many variants are kept warm. The TCG plugin lifecycle is not designed for clean pause/resume across multiple ROIs in one process (per-region state would accumulate). IPC adds a new failure surface that the file-and-fd contract does not.
- Why rejected: the dominant cost is vsim's cycle-accurate window itself, not the inter-region spawn overhead. A 1-2 s spawn against a 100-second ROI is rounding error. The file-and-fd contract is debuggable (the state file is inspectable, the mmap is inspectable); IPC handshakes are not.

### Alternative B: In-process coexistence (QEMU-as-library + vsim-as-library)

- Approach: link QEMU's TCG runtime and vsim's Verilator runtime into a single host process; switch is a function call. This is the literal gem5 shape.
- Pros: zero spawn cost; uniform memory; same address space for both models; no serialization round-trip needed for non-architectural state.
- Cons: QEMU is not designed to be linked as a library against arbitrary hosts - `-shared` builds exist but are fragile against plugin-API expectations. vsim cannot be shut down and restarted within the same process without leaking SystemC kernel state. Every CPU variant in `hybrid_vsim/src/cpu_wrappers/` would need a "reset to clean state without process exit" path that does not exist today. Effort is "rewrite both runtimes' lifecycle".
- Why rejected: the entire reason QEMU mainline is used as-is (no patches, no fork) is that we inherit upstream updates without integration cost. Library-mode QEMU is a fork in all but name.

### Alternative C: Per-trigger-kind orchestrators (no unification)

- Approach: keep today's split orchestrator (test harness for CSR, separate script for icount, separate tool for SimPoint replay, separate tool for checkpoint archive). Each trigger adds its own pipeline.
- Pros: zero upfront design cost; each trigger ships independently when its workflow needs it.
- Cons: N triggers means N spawn paths, N error-handling stories, N checkpoint formats. Bug fixes in one path do not flow to the others. The current `qemu_handback.hpp` + `run_e2e.sh` split is already showing friction at N=1; adding three more pipelines will replicate the friction three times.
- Why rejected: the third caller is when the abstraction's value crystallizes. We can see four callers coming; investing in the orchestrator now is cheaper than refactoring out the divergence later.

### Alternative D: Pure-file SimPoint workflow (no live orchestrator at all)

- Approach: emit BBV in QEMU, run SimPoint offline, drain K state-files via standalone CLI invocations, replay each in vsim via standalone CLI invocations. The user composes shell commands; there is no process named "orchestrator".
- Pros: matches existing shell-script ergonomics; minimum mechanism; nothing to maintain beyond the plugin and vsim.
- Cons: every regression in the file-handoff contract is rediscovered by every user; no single place enforces ABI version, mmap layout, or trigger-spec grammar; checkpoint archive format becomes whatever the user happens to write. Phase-3 already proved this is painful even at N=1 (state file path conventions, QEMU2 args, mmap size, all hand-wired).
- Why rejected: the orchestrator is the integration point where the four trigger kinds become one workflow. Without it, the integration lives in tribal knowledge and per-user shell scripts.

## Open Questions / TBD

- [ ] Whether the orchestrator should be a Python script (fast to iterate, depends on system Python) or a small C++ binary (fewer moving parts, shares the ABI header directly via include). Default lean: Python, with all wire-format reads going through a generated binding so the C++ ABI stays the source of truth. Owner: Nick.
- [ ] Whether BBV emission lives in the existing `hybrid_handoff` plugin or in a sibling plugin loaded in parallel. Sibling avoids feature creep but doubles the per-insn callback cost. Owner: Nick, decide at the start of the BBV-trigger phase.
- [ ] Whether checkpoint archives keep a full mmap copy or a sparse reflink. Reflink works on btrfs/xfs but falls back to sequential copy on ext4; the test infra filesystem decides. Owner: infra check before checkpoint-replay lands.
- [ ] Whether the orchestrator supports multi-hart in this iteration or treats `hart_id != 0` as a future-spec concern. The ABI struct supports it; vsim's hart-wrapper layer and the plugin's drain are single-hart today. Owner: Nick.
- [ ] Whether a "guest-driven cancel" exists - the workload signals "do *not* switch here even though the trigger fired". gem5's policy layer can refuse a trigger; nothing in hybrid_sim currently can. Owner: Nick, only if any workload genuinely needs it.
- [ ] Whether vsim needs its own icount-trigger (for "run M dynamic insns on the cycle-accurate side, then return"), or whether vsim's exit boundary always rides on a workload-embedded CSR/PC. SimPoint regions are insn-count-bounded on both ends, so a vsim icount trigger is likely required. Owner: Nick, at icount-trigger phase.

---

## Reviewer Checklist

Use this while reading. If any item is "no", leave a comment in the relevant section.

- [ ] Section 1: I understood the scope and goal in under 30 seconds.
- [ ] Section 2: I agree the problem is real and worth solving now.
- [ ] Section 3: I can draw the data flow on a whiteboard from memory after reading.
- [ ] Section 3: Coupling to existing modules is justified, not incidental.
- [ ] Section 4: The boundary is clear enough that I could implement either side independently.
- [ ] Section 5: At least one failure mode I would have raised is already addressed.
- [ ] Section 6: At least one alternative I would have asked about is already covered.
- [ ] Section 6: The trade-off framing matches reality - no strawmen.
- [ ] Open Questions: every TBD has an owner.
