# Hybrid Simulator: gem5 CPU-switch parity

> Status: Draft | Author: Nick Hung | Date: 2026-05-16 | Reviewers: self (design archive for own simulator)

## 1. 摘要與目標 (Summary & Objective)

Extend the QEMU + vsim hybrid simulator to support all four CPU-switch mechanisms described in `specs/gem5-cpu-model-switch.md` (instruction-count marker, phase-boundary signal, checkpoint completion, guest-driven request) on a single ISA (RV64). Today only the guest-driven path is wired, and the pipeline is forced linear (QEMU1 -> vsim -> QEMU2). The output is a design archive that records what shape of redesign keeps QEMU as a mainline binary, keeps vsim untouched on the cycle-accurate path, and makes new trigger sources additive rather than disruptive.

## 2. 背景與痛點 (Background & Problem Statement)

The hybrid simulator runs a workload in QEMU (functional, MHz) until the user hits a trigger, drains architectural state, replays the same state into vsim (cycle-accurate, ~5-9 KHz), runs the region of interest, drains again, and hands back to a second QEMU. This is one of gem5's four switch shapes: guest-driven request via `csrwi 0x7C0, 0/1`. Three other shapes are missing:

- **Instruction-count switch.** Workloads that do not embed the magic CSR (third-party binaries, legacy ELFs, JIT-generated code) cannot be cut at a precise dynamic-instruction offset. The TCG plugin sees every retired instruction but has no counter-trigger today. vsim's retire signal taps fire per commit but no per-icount drain hook exists either.
- **Phase-boundary switch.** SimPoint-style sampling needs the simulator to fire on workload-phase entries, not on PC matches embedded by hand. BBV emission and the trigger that consumes the cluster file do not exist. `docs/simpoint_transport_comparison.md` already recommends the shared-mmap transport for this; the trigger surface itself is still missing.
- **Checkpoint replay.** A snapshot taken under QEMU cannot be replayed independently of the run that produced it. Today the snapshot is a transient `state.bin` consumed by the next process in the pipeline; there is no archive format, no metadata, and no entry point that says "start vsim from this archived snapshot".

A second mismatch is orthogonal: the current pipeline is **linear and one-shot**. There is no way to switch QEMU -> vsim -> QEMU -> vsim ... N times in a single run. SimPoint with K=10 regions needs K independent (snapshot, replay) pairs and today's orchestrator (a shell script that wires three exec calls) cannot express it.

The compound effect: every workflow that wants more than "one fast prefix and one slow ROI" - phase sampling, multi-region SimPoint, retroactive analysis of an archived snapshot, flip-flop comparison - hits the same shortfall. Adding each capability as an isolated patch creates a different control-flow shape per use case. Recording the unified design now is cheaper than discovering it three projects from now.

## 3. 架構設計與核心邏輯 (Architecture & Core Logic)

### 3.1 The three roles (mapped onto QEMU+vsim)

```
                +--------------------------------+
                |   policy layer                 |
                |   (decides WHEN to switch)     |
                |                                |
                |   trigger sources:             |
                |   - CSR write       (today)    |
                |   - ELF symbol PC   (today)    |
                |   - dynamic icount  (new)      |
                |   - BBV phase index (new)      |
                |   - checkpoint load (new)      |
                +-------------+------------------+
                              |
                              v
                +--------------------------------+
                |   switch orchestrator          |
                |   (executes the swap protocol) |
                |                                |
                |   today: run_e2e.sh +          |
                |          qemu_handback (split) |
                |   new:   single session driver |
                |          owns mmap fd + plan   |
                +-------------+------------------+
                              |
              +---------------+---------------+
              v                               v
        +------------+                  +------------+
        |  QEMU      |  --- handoff --->|  vsim      |
        | (process)  |                  | (process)  |
        +-----+------+                  +------+-----+
              |                                |
              +-------------+------------------+
                            v
        +----------------------------------------+
        | shared simulated machine               |
        |   /dev/shm guest RAM (mmap)            |
        |   V scratch at GPA 0x80FF0000          |
        |   hybrid_state_v1 file (per handoff)   |
        +----------------------------------------+
```

Three roles, three layers. Today the orchestrator is fused with the policy layer inside the plugin (the plugin both decides "the CSR fired" and "drain now"). The redesign separates them: the policy layer produces *signals*, the orchestrator decides *what happens next*. That separation is the precondition for adding new triggers without forking the spawn path.

### 3.2 The "co-existence" decision, mapped to subprocesses

gem5 keeps both CPU instances live and parks the dormant one in-place. We cannot do that here: an idle vsim process holds GB-scale Verilator state plus a SystemC kernel; keeping it warm across a 10-minute QEMU prefix is wasteful, and multiplying that by N coexisting variants is worse.

The hybrid sim's substitute is **persistent shared state + ephemeral processes**:

- The **simulated machine** lives outside any CPU process: it is the shared mmap (guest RAM + V scratch region). It is born when the orchestrator creates the backing file and persists for the whole session.
- A **CPU process** (QEMU or vsim) is spawned when needed, runs until its trigger fires, drains to a state file, and exits. The orchestrator inherits its exit code and the populated state file.
- "Active vs dormant" maps to "running vs not-yet-spawned (or exited)". Switching from one model to the other is: exit the current process, spawn the next one, hand it the state file plus the mmap.

Corollaries (the same shape as gem5's, with the substitution):

- A region is born with a chosen first model; the orchestrator picks up front, not the binary.
- Switching back is the same operation: exit the current process, spawn the other model, pass it the same state file format. Direction is not baked in.
- N-way switching is "N spawns" with one mmap that outlives all of them. No new mechanism beyond persistent mmap + serial spawn.

This converts "can the dormant CPU survive while the active one runs?" into "can the mmap survive between two consecutive spawns?". The latter is trivially yes - it is a `/dev/shm` file held by an fd open in the orchestrator. The trade-off we pay vs gem5: a 1-2 s spawn cost per switch instead of a function call. Against a 100-second cycle-accurate window, that is rounding error; against a 1-minute SimPoint replay batch of 10 regions, it is 10-20 s of overhead total.

### 3.3 What is preserved vs. what is replaced

| Class | Where it lives | Survives switch? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Guest RAM | `/dev/shm` mmap | Yes (zero-copy) | mmap fd held by orchestrator across spawns |
| Architectural regs / PC / CSRs | `hybrid_state_v1` file | Yes (file is the contract) | Per-thread; ABI-locked |
| V regfile | `state.vreg[]` + V scratch GPA `0x80FF0000` | Yes (file + scratch) | Fixture-aware contract: vsim writes scratch, fixture issues `vle8.v` |
| FPRs, M-mode CSRs, PMP | `hybrid_state_v1` file | Yes (passthrough) | vsim does not re-read these; they ride QEMU1 -> QEMU2 unchanged |
| QEMU TCG TLB / softmmu cache | QEMU process memory | No | New QEMU spawn starts cold |
| vsim BPU / caches / TLB / store buffer | vsim process memory | No | New vsim spawn starts cold; kanata-loop-skip warms |

Microarchitectural state is intentionally discarded at every switch boundary, consistent with gem5's "TLB is CPU-private" rule. The kanata-loop-skip mechanism (already in vsim) is the documented warmup window before the recorded slice. The deliberate cost of the persistent-state-plus-ephemeral-process model is repeated cold start per ROI; the deliberate benefit is no in-process CPU teardown logic.

### 3.4 The swap protocol (the shape already exists)

The three-phase shape is implemented today; making it explicit lets new triggers slot in without case-by-case changes.

1. **Quiesce.** The CPU process commits its in-flight work and returns to a documented "at rest" point. For QEMU, this is `qemu_plugin_request_exit()` after the plugin has read every register. For vsim, this is `StateDrain` after a DMI halt and `dret`. No timed events outlive the process exit; no architectural fact survives without being either in the state file or in the mmap.
2. **Hand off.** The orchestrator (a) collects the exited process's state file, (b) optionally rewrites it (checkpoint case: load from archive instead of from the just-exited child), (c) decides the next model. The mmap is reused; it is not closed or copied. The state file is the only architectural artifact that crosses the boundary.
3. **Resume.** The orchestrator spawns the next process with the state file as input. QEMU consumes the file via gdbstub `G`/`P` packets before `continue`; vsim consumes it via `ResumeDriver` (DM Abstract Commands + Program Buffer + V scratch). Execution continues from the same PC; the simulated clock does not advance.

The protocol is uniform regardless of trigger kind. A new trigger source adds a *reason* the process exits, not a new exit path.

### 3.5 Why a universal quiescence contract (already true, almost by accident)

Both processes exit cleanly at switch time. The TCG plugin's `request_exit` flushes QEMU's TB cache and returns through the main loop. vsim's `StateDrain` halts the hart via DMI and returns architectural state without leaving Verilator in an intermediate state. No bespoke per-trigger drain logic exists or is needed.

What changes with new triggers: the *cause* of the quiesce is different (icount overflow vs CSR write vs phase-index match vs orchestrator-injected exit signal). The drain path is the same. The orchestrator polls "did the process exit cleanly with code 200?" and reads the state file. A new trigger ships as "an additional reason to set `should_exit = true` inside the plugin"; nothing else moves.

### 3.6 Trigger sources (the deliverable)

Four trigger sources, each implementable independently against the same orchestrator contract:

- **Guest-driven request** (today). `csrwi 0x7C0, 0/1` or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with the runtime kind. Both QEMU plugin and vsim's `CsrTrigger` already implement; the ELF can also embed an `_hybrid_enter_pc`/`_hybrid_exit_pc` symbol for PC-mode. No change.

- **Instruction-count marker** (additive). Both QEMU and vsim gain a per-insn counter and a `--switch-at-icount N` argument. QEMU's TCG plugin already sees every retired insn; the counter is a single uint64 incremented in the per-insn callback. vsim's retire signal tap (`common_signals_45.hpp`) already fires per commit; a sibling trigger class IcountTrigger sits alongside the existing CsrTrigger and votes for drain at commit time. Wire format unchanged.

- **Phase-boundary signal** (BBV-driven). The TCG plugin emits BBVs every fixed slice (e.g., 100M dynamic insns) into a `.bb` file during a profiling pass. An offline SimPoint clustering pass selects K representative slice indices and weights. A second plugin run consumes the slice-index file and fires the drain trigger when the dynamic insn counter reaches each chosen slice. Mechanically this is the icount trigger with a slice-aware policy on top - the same `should_exit` mechanism, a different policy that decides when to set it. SimPoint clustering is offline and out of scope for the plugin.

- **Checkpoint completion** (orchestrator-side). The orchestrator persists, per switch boundary, both the `state.bin` and a snapshot of the mmap backing file into `snap/<N>/{state.bin, mem}`. To replay a checkpoint, the orchestrator points a new mmap at `snap/<N>/mem`, copies `snap/<N>/state.bin` into the working state-file path, and spawns vsim (or QEMU) from there. No plugin or vsim change is needed; the orchestrator gains a `replay-checkpoint <dir>` entry point and a snapshot policy.

All four reduce to the same downstream: "a process exits cleanly with a populated state file; the mmap holds the rest". The orchestrator does not need to know *why* the process exited, only that it did, and what to do next per the session plan.

### 3.7 The orchestrator (the missing piece)

Today the orchestrator is split: `run_e2e.sh` for tests, `qemu_handback.hpp` for spawning QEMU2, plugin args + CLI flags for direction. There is no single entry point that owns "run a session of N switches".

The redesign adds one program (script or small C++ binary) that:

- Owns the mmap fd for the whole session and passes it (or its path) to each spawn.
- Holds the trigger plan: an ordered list of `(model, trigger_spec)` pairs that says, in effect, "first run QEMU until icount=5e9, then vsim until next CSR exit, then QEMU until end-of-program; on each boundary, persist a checkpoint into snap/N/".
- Spawns one process at a time per the plan; after each exit, reads the state file, optionally archives it, and consults the plan for the next action.
- Surfaces a `replay-checkpoint` mode that pre-populates the mmap and the state file from an archived directory before spawning, instead of starting from the original ELF.

The orchestrator is the only place that needs to know about all four trigger sources. The plugin and vsim see only the kind that applies to them per spawn.

## 4. 介面與資料合約 (Interface & Data Contract)

Four contracts, three of which already exist or are nearly there. The fourth (orchestrator -> CPU) is what the redesign formalizes.

- **CPU model contract.** Each CPU process accepts an input state (a state file plus a shared-mmap path), runs until its drain trigger fires, writes a state file on the way out, and exits with a documented code (0 = workload completed, 200 = drain-and-handoff, non-zero non-200 = failure). vsim already does this; QEMU does it through the plugin's `request_exit`. Each model owns its in-process trigger registration (TCG per-insn callbacks; vsim's CsrTrigger / future IcountTrigger). A model that cannot participate (e.g., a KVM-accelerated QEMU, where the plugin cannot intercept) opts out by failing the orchestrator's pre-spawn check before any side effect occurs.

- **Per-thread architectural state contract.** `hybrid_state_v1` (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`), already byte-locked by `state_abi_check.h`. The redesign does not change the wire format; new triggers do not need new fields. Multi-hart is structurally represented (`hart_id`) though only single-hart workloads are exercised today. The struct's `flags` already gate which state blocks are live; future trigger metadata, if needed at all, lands in the `reserved` window.

- **Shared-machine contract.** Guest RAM lives in one `/dev/shm` file backed by `memory-backend-file,share=on` on the QEMU side and by `mmap_backing.hpp` on the vsim side. The orchestrator owns the fd; spawned processes inherit it by path. V regfile transport uses a fixed GPA (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`) inside the same mmap. The contract is "every byte at GPA X reads identically on both sides at boundary moments"; vsim's `simple_memory.hpp` redesign (hybrid_plan.md Section 9) made that true by aligning vsim's RAM region with QEMU virt's RAM base.

- **Orchestrator -> CPU contract** (new). A spawned process is invoked with a uniform set of args:
  - `--state-in <path>` (read at startup) or `--cold-start` for the session's first QEMU.
  - `--state-out <path>` (written on drain).
  - `--mem-path <path>` (mmap; shared across spawns; size derived from the file).
  - `--trigger-spec <opaque>` - per-trigger-kind arguments. CSR is the default; PC/icount/phase pass enough to identify the firing condition (the specific PC, the icount target, the slice-index list).

  The CPU does not know about other CPUs or other switches in the session. The orchestrator owns sequencing.

Under the subprocess model, gem5's "connection rebinding" and "quiescence" contracts collapse into the orchestrator-CPU contract: rebinding is "the orchestrator passes the same mmap path to the next spawn"; quiescence is "the process exited with code 200 and the state file passes magic+version checks". The load-bearing boundary is this new one; once it stabilizes, adding a trigger is "extend the trigger-spec grammar plus add a callback to the plugin or to vsim".

## 5. 邊界條件與例外處理 (Edge Cases & Error Handling)

| Scenario | Likelihood | Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two trigger conditions fire on the same retired insn (e.g., icount + CSR) | Medium | Plugin / vsim resolves with a fixed priority: CSR/PC > phase > icount; only the highest-priority is honored | Multi-fire on the same boundary doubles state files; one rule, all triggers |
| Plugin or vsim exits without writing a complete state file | Low | Orchestrator validates magic, version, and exact size before acting; mismatched -> session fails immediately | Silent truncation is the worst-case bug; visible failure here is cheaper than a corrupt replay |
| Checkpoint replay against a mmap whose backing file is missing or has the wrong size | Medium (archived sessions) | Orchestrator verifies file presence and size against archived metadata before any spawn | Checkpoint archives are the long-lived artifact; broken archives must be obvious at load time, not at retire time |
| Snapshot taken on one CPU variant, replayed on another | Low | ABI struct's `flags` identifies live state classes; orchestrator rejects when target variant cannot consume them (e.g., `HYBRID_FLAG_V` set, target lacks V) | The wire format does not know what CPU is consuming it; the orchestrator does |
| Phase-trigger fires while QEMU is mid-TB | Medium (BBV) | Plugin always defers the drain to the next retired-insn boundary; never preempts TB-internal | Consistent with how CSR/PC triggers already work; one rule, all triggers |
| N-way switch with V-state and stale V scratch from a previous spawn | Low | Each spawn writes V scratch from its `state.vreg[]` before the fixture issues any `vle8.v`; previous bytes are overwritten | Scratch is single-writer per spawn; orchestrator does not need to clear it |
| Stale `state.bin` from a previous session sharing the same /tmp path | Low | Orchestrator writes all per-session files into a session-scoped `mktemp -d`; no path is reused across sessions | Path collisions across runs would silently feed bad data |
| Trigger fires on a hart that does not exist (`hart_id` out of range) | Low | Orchestrator rejects at plan-validation time, before the first spawn | Cheap to check up front; expensive to debug if missed mid-session |
| KVM/HVF-accelerated QEMU in the spawn list | Out of scope | Orchestrator refuses such a spawn at plan-validation; document the requirement | Accel modes bypass TCG and the plugin API; no drain primitive exists |
| MMIO touched inside a vsim ROI | Out of scope | Existing interconnect-watch (Phase 5 of hybrid_plan.md) fails loudly | Device state has no mirror in RTL; orthogonal to switch mechanism |
| Cross-ISA switch (e.g., RV64 -> RV32) | Out of scope | ABI struct is RV64-only; no translation contract exists | Different design problem; not in this spec |

The two failure modes that bit us during Phase-3 e2e (truncated state file, mmap size mismatch) are already in the test matrix (`rt_corrupt_state.sh`, the `simple_memory.hpp` size-check landed by hybrid_plan.md Section 9). New triggers do not introduce new failure modes; they reuse the existing quiesce/spawn pair.

## 6. 替代方案與權衡 (Alternatives Considered & Trade-offs)

### Alternative A: Persistent-process orchestrator (gem5-style "both alive")

- Approach: never exit either CPU process. Keep QEMU and vsim both running in paused states; switching becomes "tell one to drain, tell the other to resume" over IPC (Unix socket, shared memory queue).
- Pros: avoids the 1-2 s spawn cost per switch; closer to gem5's literal "both CPUs co-resident" model; trivial flip-flop sampling between two paused models.
- Cons: a paused vsim still holds GB of Verilator state; a paused QEMU still holds its TB cache and softmmu translations. Memory cost is multiplied by however many variants are kept warm. The TCG plugin lifecycle is not designed for clean pause/resume across multiple ROIs in one process (per-region state would accumulate). IPC adds a new failure surface that the file-and-fd contract does not.
- Why rejected: the dominant cost is vsim's cycle-accurate window itself, not the inter-region spawn overhead. A 1-2 s spawn against a 100-second ROI is rounding error. The file-and-fd contract is debuggable (the state file is inspectable, the mmap is inspectable); IPC handshakes are not.

### Alternative B: In-process coexistence (QEMU-as-library + vsim-as-library)

- Approach: link QEMU's TCG runtime and vsim's Verilator runtime into a single host process; switch is a function call. This is the literal gem5 shape.
- Pros: zero spawn cost; uniform memory; same address space for both models; no serialization round-trip needed for non-architectural state.
- Cons: QEMU is not designed to be linked as a library against arbitrary hosts - `-shared` builds exist but are fragile against plugin-API expectations. vsim cannot be shut down and restarted within the same process without leaking SystemC kernel state. Every CPU variant in `hybrid_vsim/src/cpu_wrappers/` would need a "reset to clean state without process exit" path that does not exist today. Effort is "rewrite both runtimes' lifecycle".
- Why rejected: the entire reason QEMU mainline is used as-is (no patches, no fork) is that we inherit upstream updates without integration cost. Library-mode QEMU is a fork in all but name.

### Alternative C: Per-trigger-kind orchestrators (no unification)

- Approach: keep today's split orchestrator (test harness for CSR, separate script for icount, separate tool for SimPoint replay, separate tool for checkpoint archive). Each trigger adds its own pipeline.
- Pros: zero upfront design cost; each trigger ships independently when its workflow needs it.
- Cons: N triggers means N spawn paths, N error-handling stories, N checkpoint formats. Bug fixes in one path do not flow to the others. The current `qemu_handback.hpp` + `run_e2e.sh` split is already showing friction at N=1; adding three more pipelines will replicate the friction three times.
- Why rejected: the third caller is when the abstraction's value crystallizes. We can see four callers coming; investing in the orchestrator now is cheaper than refactoring out the divergence later.

### Alternative D: Pure-file SimPoint workflow (no live orchestrator at all)

- Approach: emit BBV in QEMU, run SimPoint offline, drain K state-files via standalone CLI invocations, replay each in vsim via standalone CLI invocations. The user composes shell commands; there is no process named "orchestrator".
- Pros: matches existing shell-script ergonomics; minimum mechanism; nothing to maintain beyond the plugin and vsim.
- Cons: every regression in the file-handoff contract is rediscovered by every user; no single place enforces ABI version, mmap layout, or trigger-spec grammar; checkpoint archive format becomes whatever the user happens to write. Phase-3 already proved this is painful even at N=1 (state file path conventions, QEMU2 args, mmap size, all hand-wired).
- Why rejected: the orchestrator is the integration point where the four trigger kinds become one workflow. Without it, the integration lives in tribal knowledge and per-user shell scripts.

## Open Questions / TBD

- [ ] Whether the orchestrator should be a Python script (fast to iterate, depends on system Python) or a small C++ binary (fewer moving parts, shares the ABI header directly via include). Default lean: Python, with all wire-format reads going through a generated binding so the C++ ABI stays the source of truth. Owner: Nick.
- [ ] Whether BBV emission lives in the existing `hybrid_handoff` plugin or in a sibling plugin loaded in parallel. Sibling avoids feature creep but doubles the per-insn callback cost. Owner: Nick, decide at the start of the BBV-trigger phase.
- [ ] Whether checkpoint archives keep a full mmap copy or a sparse reflink. Reflink works on btrfs/xfs but falls back to sequential copy on ext4; the test infra filesystem decides. Owner: infra check before checkpoint-replay lands.
- [ ] Whether the orchestrator supports multi-hart in this iteration or treats `hart_id != 0` as a future-spec concern. The ABI struct supports it; vsim's hart-wrapper layer and the plugin's drain are single-hart today. Owner: Nick.
- [ ] Whether a "guest-driven cancel" exists - the workload signals "do *not* switch here even though the trigger fired". gem5's policy layer can refuse a trigger; nothing in hybrid_sim currently can. Owner: Nick, only if any workload genuinely needs it.
- [ ] Whether vsim needs its own icount-trigger (for "run M dynamic insns on the cycle-accurate side, then return"), or whether vsim's exit boundary always rides on a workload-embedded CSR/PC. SimPoint regions are insn-count-bounded on both ends, so a vsim icount trigger is likely required. Owner: Nick, at icount-trigger phase.

Spec saved to `./specs/hybrid-sim-gem5-switch-parity.md`. Please review before I start implementing.

- **Problem:** hybrid_sim implements only one of gem5's four switch mechanisms (guest-driven CSR write); icount, phase-boundary (SimPoint), and checkpoint-replay are missing, and the pipeline is forced linear/one-shot.
- **Proposed approach:** keep QEMU mainline + vsim untouched; substitute gem5's in-process coexistence with **persistent shared mmap + ephemeral CPU processes**; add a single session orchestrator that owns the mmap fd, the trigger plan, and N spawns.
- **Top alternative rejected:** persistent-process orchestrator (Alt A) - 1-2 s spawn cost is negligible against 100-s cycle-accurate ROIs, and the file-and-fd contract is more debuggable than IPC.
- **Open TBDs:** Python vs C++ orchestrator; BBV plugin colocation; checkpoint archive format (reflink vs full copy); multi-hart scope; vsim icount-trigger requirement; guest-driven cancel.
